arguments.task: Open the official Threads developer documentation and read the page contents. Then navigate only to first-party official pages needed to verify: what the Threads API does, documented authentication methods, whether access is self-serve or gated, API style/surface, rate limits, and whether official docs mention an MCP/server. Prefer canonical developer/help/business/pricing pages that render content on plain GET when possible. Return a concise structured summary with the exact URLs actually visited and used as evidence.

arguments.task: Open and read only official first-party pages for Threads API and return a final structured extraction. Visit canonical developer pages that work in-browser for: overview, get started/authentication, reference, rate limits, and app review/permissions. Also check whether there is any official Meta page mentioning an MCP server for Threads. For each visited page, capture the exact URL and the specific evidence. Stop once you can decisively answer description, auth methods, access tier, API surface type/breadth, rate limits, and MCP existence.

raw_result.description: Meta’s Threads API lets apps publish posts, read profiles and conversation content, fetch insights, manage replies, receive webhooks, and embed Threads posts.
raw_result.auth_notes: The official Threads get-started docs describe OAuth 2.0 login/authorization and the use of user access tokens, including short-lived and long-lived tokens.
raw_result.gating_reason: Official docs indicate developers can create a Threads-enabled Meta app and test with app roles/testers, but public/production access to requested permissions requires App Review approval.

api_surface.breadth: Official documentation shows a Graph API-style surface at graph.threads.com with multiple endpoints for publishing, profiles, replies, insights, webhooks, and oEmbed, but the gathered evidence is not strong enough to cleanly separate whether this should be classified strictly as GraphQL versus REST in the required schema.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No official Meta Threads documentation was found in the gathered evidence that mentions an MCP server.

raw_result.blocker: Production use requires Meta App Review approval for permissions beyond local/test-role access.
raw_result.rate_limit_notes: The gathered reference-page evidence mentions a publishing limit on the user/reference surface, but no verified numeric limit was captured from a canonical page during this pass.
raw_result.notable_signals: Official docs present Threads as part of the Meta developer ecosystem using a Threads use case, permissioned scopes such as threads_basic and publishing-related permissions, and a dedicated reference hosted on graph.threads.com documentation pages.
raw_result.human_review_reason: Structured fields remain uncertain from the gathered evidence: api_surface.type is ambiguous in the required schema because Meta documents the Threads API as a Graph API surface rather than clearly REST or GraphQL, and rate-limit specifics were not captured from a fully read canonical page.
